title: Depressive States
description: Overcoming depressive states and developing resilience.
welcomeMessage: Find a path out of depressive state. Master practical techniques for restoring energy and interest in life.

cards.introduction: When your tank is empty, your mind often talks to you in short, harsh lines: “pull yourself together”, “stop being weak”, “what’s wrong with you”. On the outside you may still function—work, reply, do what’s necessary—while on the inside everything feels heavy and flat. That’s not laziness; it’s an overloaded nervous system trying to save energy. The first step isn’t heroics—it’s clarity: noticing what your brain runs on autopilot. In this card we’ll give your state a clear shape so it becomes more manageable.
cards.shortDescription: When energy is low, self-talk can turn brutal. Naming automatic thoughts creates the first bit of control and relief.
questions: Think back over the last 7 days: when did apathy/helplessness hit the hardest? Write down 2–3 concrete moments. Example: “after work, sitting in the car, I didn’t even want to go home”. Rate intensity 1–10 and add what you felt in your body (heaviness, numbness, sleepiness).

What did you say to yourself in those moments—word for word? Write 2–3 lines. Example: “it’s pointless”, “I can’t handle anything”. How did those lines affect your next move: freeze, procrastinate, snap, scroll?
cards.recommendation:
When a thought feels like “the truth”, you act as if you have no choice. When you can see it as a thought, a small gap opens up—and with it, a small choice. That reduces fusion with self-criticism and brings back a sense of agency.

Do this:
1) For 7 days, catch 1 moment a day when you feel that “drop” (it doesn’t have to be the worst moment).
2) Write three short items: situation (where/with whom), thought (word for word), action (what you did or didn’t do).
3) Rate 1–10 how much you believed the thought in that moment.
4) Add one separating line: “I’m having the thought that…” (don’t argue with it—just label it).

Example: “After a meeting. Thought: ‘I don’t matter.’ Action: I sat and scrolled. Belief 8/10. I’m having the thought that I don’t matter.”

If it feels awkward, that’s normal—especially if you’re used to keeping a strong face. The goal isn’t inspiration; it’s seeing the autopilot. That’s enough for level 1.
cards.mechanism: Reducing fusion with automatic thoughts and self-criticism.

cards.introduction: “I don’t want anything” can look like laziness, but inside it’s often protection from pain and overload. When energy is low, your brain chooses the safest mode: don’t start, so you won’t face disappointment, criticism, or that familiar “I failed again” feeling. Many men get stuck here because we’re taught to stay composed—and it feels shameful to admit you’re running on zero. The way out isn’t a big push. It’s a micro‑move that proves: “I can still act.” In this card we’ll make the start small enough to be real today.
cards.shortDescription: Apathy isn’t always laziness—it can be your system protecting you. Micro-steps restore agency and restart momentum.
questions: Over the last week, what have you postponed most? Write 3 items: one household, one work, one health/relationship. Example: “didn’t reply to an email”, “didn’t book a doctor”, “avoided a conversation”.

When you look at that list, what’s stronger inside—exhaustion, anxiety, shame, anger, numbness? Rate 1–10 and add the first thought that shows up. Example: “if I start, I’ll realise I can’t handle it”.
cards.recommendation:
In a depressive state, motivation usually doesn’t come first. More often it’s the other way around: a small action creates a bit of energy, and then motivation follows. That’s behavioural activation.

Do this:
1) Pick one item that’s unpleasant but important (not the biggest one).
2) Break it into a step that takes 5 minutes or less.
3) Set a 5‑minute timer and do only that step. Stop.
4) Mark it with one line: “I did the minimum possible.” Rate heaviness 1–10 before/after.

Example: “Email I avoided for a week. Step: open inbox and draft 2 sentences. Timer 5 minutes. After: heaviness 4/10 instead of 6/10.”

If your mind says “this is ridiculous”, that’s exactly why it works: we’re fitting the task to your real capacity, not forcing hero mode.
cards.mechanism: Small activation reduces helplessness and restores a sense of control.

cards.introduction: In low mood, thoughts can sound like facts: “it’s over”, “I’m useless”, “nothing will change”. The problem is that your brain filters reality in that mode: it highlights mistakes and ignores anything that went even slightly okay. If you grew up with “handle it yourself”, those thoughts also hit your dignity—like you’re failing as a man. But a thought is a story, not a verdict. In this card we’ll practise separating facts from interpretations and building a more honest picture.
cards.shortDescription: In depression, thoughts easily feel like facts. We’ll practise reality-checking them to reduce inner pressure.
questions: Recall the last time you were hit hard (within 7–10 days). What was the main thought about yourself/life? Write it word for word. How much did you believe it (1–10)?
What 2–3 facts contradict that thought even slightly? Facts are camera‑proof: actions, numbers, messages, real events.
cards.recommendation:
Adding facts pulls your brain out of black‑and‑white “truth mode”. It reduces catastrophising and builds grounded perspective—without fake positivity.

Do this (5–7 minutes):
1) Write the punch‑thought (one sentence).
2) Label the distortion (all‑or‑nothing, overgeneralising, mind‑reading, catastrophising).
3) Write 3 facts “for” and 3 facts “against”.
4) Write a more accurate thought that includes the facts.

Example: “I’m a loser.” Facts against: “I finished 2 tasks”, “helped my mum”, “didn’t snap at my kid even though I was boiling”. New thought: “I’m exhausted, so my mind goes black‑and‑white. I’m not perfect, but I keep doing important things.”

If you hear “this won’t help”—treat it as another thought and return to facts.
cards.mechanism: Separating facts from interpretations weakens negative beliefs and reduces distress.

cards.introduction: We often devalue what we do — even if it's important.
cards.shortDescription: We often devalue what we do — even if it's important.
questions: What did you accomplish this week despite fatigue or resistance?
How do you usually react to such efforts — acknowledge or ignore?
cards.recommendation:
When your brain doesn’t register completions, life feels like an endless grind. Recording what you did restores the link “I act → it matters”.

Do this (2 minutes in the evening):
1) Write 3 completions from today (one can be very small).
2) Add the cost: what it took (time, effort, patience).
3) Lock it in with a no‑drama respect line: “That was hard, and I did it.”

Example: “Went shopping—effort 7/10. Made one work call—6/10. Played with my kid for 10 minutes—5/10.”

If “self‑praise is weak” pops up—treat this as a factual report, not ego inflation.
cards.mechanism: Positive reinforcement supports motivation and sustained action.

cards.introduction: Hopelessness often hits not because everything is objectively doomed, but because your mind tries to hold too much at once. Work, money, health, relationships, the future—one big knot, and it feels like there’s nowhere to turn. Many men go into control mode: “I have to fix everything”—and that’s exactly what breaks you. The way out is to narrow the scale to what’s manageable and reclaim a small area of influence. In this card we’ll turn “everything” into one concrete step for today.
cards.shortDescription: When your mind holds “everything at once”, hopelessness grows. We’ll narrow focus to one small, controllable step.
questions: What 3 areas feel most “stuck” right now? Rate pressure 1–10 for each.
If you could change each by 1%, what would that look like? Write one micro‑action per area (up to 10 minutes).
cards.recommendation:
One small step tells your brain: “I’m not completely powerless.” That reduces chaos and helps you exit paralysis.

Do this:
1) Pick the one area with the highest pressure.
2) Define a 10‑minute action you can do today even if mood is “zero”.
3) Drop perfection: the goal is not “solve”, it’s “shift”.
4) Do it and write one line: what moved by 1%?

If your mind says “too small”, that’s the depression trap: all‑or‑nothing. Today we train “something”.
cards.mechanism: Narrowing focus to controllable actions reduces helplessness and overwhelm.

cards.introduction: In depression, your brain often tunes to one channel: the bad is sharp, the neutral and good don’t “count”. That’s not your personality—it’s an attention habit amplified by stress and exhaustion. For many men it also hits identity: if you don’t feel joy, you start thinking you’re “empty” or “broken”. But attention can be trained like a muscle—small reps, no forcing. In this card we’ll practise noticing neutral/good signals without pretending to be optimistic.
cards.shortDescription: Depression biases attention toward the negative. We’ll train noticing neutral/good facts to restore balance.
questions: Over the last 3 days, name 3 neutral or good facts (even tiny). If “nothing”, write what blocks you from noticing.
What did you do that helped these facts happen—even by 1%?
cards.recommendation:
We’re not training “positive thinking”. We’re training accurate attention. When your brain starts seeing a link between a small effort and a small improvement, it becomes easier to keep going.

Do this (5 days in a row, 3 minutes):
1) Write one “neutral/good fact of the day” (a fact, not a feeling).
2) Write one action of yours that contributed to it.
3) Rate 1–10 how hard it was to notice.

If your mind says “these are crumbs”—yes, they are. That’s how the brain relearns life: one small signal at a time.
cards.mechanism: Training attention to neutral/positive cues weakens the negative filter.

cards.introduction: When it’s heavy, you may want to disappear: “I don’t want to burden anyone”, “I’ll handle it alone”. For men, that can even look “dignified”. But isolation often makes depression worse: your inner critic gets louder, and your body gets no relief through human contact. Important: contact doesn’t mean a one‑hour emotional confession. Sometimes it’s a small, human check‑in. In this card we’ll choose a safe format of connection you can do within 48 hours.
cards.shortDescription: Isolation can deepen depression even if it feels “right”. We’ll choose a small, safe contact you can do within 48 hours.
questions: Who feels easiest to breathe around—less judgment, less pressure? Write 2–3 people (or the least hard one).
What format is doable right now: text, a 5‑minute call, a walk, doing something together? Rate awkwardness/fear 1–10.
cards.recommendation:
Connection signals safety to your nervous system. This isn’t about being cheerful—it’s about not being alone inside the heaviness.

Do this:
1) Pick one person and the simplest 5–20 minute format.
2) Make a no‑shame, no‑long‑story request.
3) Schedule it: today/tomorrow, time, channel.

Text templates:
- “I’m running on empty. Can we talk for 10 minutes—no advice, just a check‑in?”
- “Want to take a short walk? 20 minutes.”

Asking for a small contact is maturity, not defeat.
cards.mechanism: Social contact supports emotional regulation and reduces perceived threat.

cards.introduction: Depression can cut off not only joy, but also your sense of “who I am”. Life turns into work‑home‑sleep, and you feel like you’re living on autopilot. For many men this hits identity hard: if I’m not effective, not “in shape”, not interesting—then who am I? The way back isn’t inspiration; it’s practice: identity is built through value‑based actions. In this card we’ll gently bring back one small “thread” of you—without demanding you feel excited.
cards.shortDescription: When you lose contact with yourself, one small value-based action can reconnect you. We’ll restore one “thread” of identity without pressure.
questions: What 3 things used to be “you” before it got heavy (activities or qualities)?
What’s the easiest to bring back for 10 minutes within the next 3 days? Rate feasibility 1–10.
cards.recommendation:
We’re restoring direction, not “fun”. Even 10 minutes of action aligned with your values strengthens identity and meaning.

Do this:
1) Pick one thread (health, skill, relationships, creativity).
2) Choose a 10‑minute minimum step.
3) Do it 3 times in 3 days.
4) After each time, write one line: “Who was I in that moment?”

If pleasure doesn’t show up, that’s normal. We’re building the path back, not forcing mood.
cards.mechanism: Value-based actions strengthen identity and reduce emptiness.

cards.introduction: On the hardest days you may want to wait until you “feel motivated” to start living again. But depression often survives on that waiting: fewer actions → less energy → more waiting. For men, this can be extra painful because the usual tactic—“just pull yourself together”—stops working and turns into shame. You need a different logic: not willpower, but a tiny pre‑planned action. In this card we’ll pick one 10‑minute task and make a clear commitment for the next 48 hours.
cards.shortDescription: Motivation often comes after action, not before. We’ll choose one 10‑minute task and do it within 48 hours.
questions: What one task have you postponed for more than a week, but it’s doable in 10 minutes?
When exactly will you do it within the next 48 hours (day + time)? What might block you—and what’s your if‑then plan?
cards.recommendation:
We’re training the link “I act → I influence”, not “I do it perfectly”. Even a small action reduces helplessness and restores self‑trust.

Do this:
1) Define the task very specifically (what exactly is 10 minutes).
2) Set an exact time in the next 48 hours.
3) Prep the environment (open the app, place documents, draft a message).
4) Do it for 10 minutes and stop.

If your mind says “that’s nothing”, return to the fact: you did what you promised yourself. That’s adult strength in a heavy state.
cards.mechanism: Small targeted action reduces helplessness and strengthens self-trust.

cards.introduction: Depression can make it feel like your current state is you—as if it will last forever and there’s no “other version” of you. But a state is weather, not identity. For men who measure themselves by function, the thought “if I’m not pulling it, I’m worthless” hits hard. This card isn’t about motivational speeches. It’s about building a bridge from today to the kind of man you want to be—through small actions. That’s how dignity returns, even when you feel empty.
cards.shortDescription: You are more than today’s mood. We’ll choose 3 qualities of your “future self” and express them through actions over 3 days.
questions: If we remove your mood, who do you want to be in relationships/work/with yourself? Choose 3 qualities (calm, reliable, honest, caring, disciplined, alive).
What small actions (up to 10 minutes) could express each quality tomorrow?
cards.recommendation:
When you act from values rather than mood, you stop being a hostage to your state. That strengthens identity and brings meaning.

Do this (3 days):
1) Write 3 qualities: “the man I want to be”.
2) For each, choose one 5–10 minute action.
3) Each day, pick one quality and do the action.
4) End with one line: “What does this say about me?”

You don’t have to feel strong to act like an adult.
cards.mechanism: Values-based actions strengthen identity and reduce mood-driven behaviour.
